title: About
text: The dashboard and accompanying pages were built in R using Quarto and an R package developed for this purpose. Data source details, the dashboard hierarchy, and a data dictionary are each provided below.

title: Home
text:
The EF2023 Dashboard is a grouping and visual summary of Canada Energy Regulator’s raw data on future outlooks for Canadian energy.
The CER published EF2023 data in 2024 as part of the Canada’s Energy Future series in assessment of alignment with Paris Agreement targets1.
In their own words, the dataset “explores how possible energy futures might unfold for Canadians over the long term”. Data covers fuel pricing, energy trade streams, industrial activity, electrical systems, energy demand, and economic indicators.
Below is a digest of data trends to expect in the dashboard, focusing on 2023-2050 projections and attempting to estimate the scale of corrections required to achieve net-zero targets, as well as their consequences (for example, crude oil total production decreases by about -40% in a global net-zero scenario).
The About section contains information on the dataset source and further project details.

Hydrogen on the rise (+225 to +219%), with AB producing 40% on average.
Natural Gas down (-18 to -39%), with AB producing 58% on average.

### Card: Current Measures total (1/3)
v1 <- Production$Crude_Oil$Total %>%
  filter(Year > 2022,
         Region == "Canada",
         Scenario == "Current Measures") %>%
  pull(Value) %>%
  {sum(. * 1000) * 365.24}

value_box_value <- v1 %>%
  # rp_format_proper2(format = list(do_B = T)) %>%

  paste0(" m³")

value_box(theme = value_box_theme(bg = "#777", fg = "#EEE"),
        title = "Total (2023–2050)",

###


### Card: Top contributor (Region)
# Get top contributor (Region)
value_box_data <- Production$Crude_Oil$Total %>%
  filter(Region != "Canada") %>%
  # Compute averages and contributions
  summarize(Value_Total = mean(Value),
            Value_Average = mean(Value),
            .by = Region) %>%
  mutate(Value_Percentage = Value_Total / sum(Value_Total)) %>% 
  # Get highest contribution
  arrange(desc(Value_Percentage)) %>%
  head(1) %>% 
  # Prepare labels
  mutate(Line_1 = paste0("Averaging ", round(Value_Average, 1), "k m³/day"),
         Line_2 = paste0("Contributing ", round(100 * Value_Percentage, 0), "% (avg.)"))
value_box_value <- value_box_data$Region
value_box_tag_1 <- value_box_data$Line_1
value_box_tag_2 <- value_box_data$Line_2

# Prepare showcase plot
value_box_plot <- Production$Crude_Oil$Total %>% 
  filter(Region == value_box_data$Region) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Value, group = Scenario,
             col = Scenario, fill = Scenario,
             ymin = pmin(0, Value), ymax = pmax(0, Value))) +
  geom_ribbon(col = NULL, fill = "#AAA", alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_line(aes(text = paste0(round(Value), ": ", Scenario, " (", Year, ")")),
            alpha = 0.25, lwd = 0.25) +
  scale_color_discrete(palette = c("#777", "#7e3434", "#256175")) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Warning in geom_ribbon(col = NULL, fill = "#AAA", alpha = 0.1): Ignoring empty
aesthetic: `colour`.


Warning in geom_line(aes(text = paste0(round(Value), ": ", Scenario, " (", :
Ignoring unknown aesthetics: text

value_box_plot <- ggplotly(value_box_plot, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(plot_bgcolor = "rgba(1,1,1,0)", hovermode = "x",
         hoverlabel = list(font = list(family = "Onest", size = 13)),
         xaxis = list(fixedrange = TRUE), yaxis = list(fixedrange = TRUE)) %>%
  config(displayModeBar = F)

# Draw value box
value_box(theme = value_box_theme(bg = "#EEE", fg = "#777"),
        title = "Top contributor",
        showcase = value_box_plot,
        showcase_layout = "bottom",
        value = value_box_value,
        # tag("p", value_box_tag_1),
        tag("p", value_box_tag_2))









Top contributor
AB
Contributing 82% (avg.)

# Main plot
Production$Crude_Oil$Total %>%
  ridge_facet_plot(rp_X = Year, rp_Y = Value, rp_F1 = Scenario, rp_F2 = Region,
                   rp_title_main = "Canadian Crude Oil Production",
                   rp_title_Y = "Production", rp_unit_abbrev = "k m³/day",
                   rp_labs_Y = list(digits = 0, do_k = F, do_e = F),
                   rp_draw = list(points = F, peaks = T, medians = F,
                                  peaks_at = list(Y_min = F, Y_max = F,
                                                  X_values = c(2005, 2022, 2030, 2035, 2050))),
                   rp_alpha = list(ribbons = 0.35))

What do these represent?


Brent
A key global crude oil benchmark price. It is a light sweet1 grade extracted from the North Sea2.
Canadian Light Sweet (CLS)
A light, sweet blend of conventionally produced crude oils in western Canada with an API gravity of around 40 degrees3. It is typically priced at Edmonton, Alberta and is often called Edmonton Par Crude.
West Texas Intermediate (WTI)
A key North American benchmark crude oil price, it’s a light sweet grade of crude oil produced in western Texas with an API gravity of 40 degrees.
Western Canadian Select (WCS)
A blend of Canadian heavy and bitumen sour4 crude oils mixed with sweet synthetic and condensates with an API gravity of about 21 degrees. It is priced at Hardisty, Alberta and is often used as a representative price for Canadian heavy crude oils.
Source: EF20235, with added footnotes.

WTI-WCS price differential
A measure of crude oil market favorability. The interpretation for Canada is that heavy crude oil can be sold more easily if the differential is narrow6, indicating a demand strong enough to offset heavy refining costs.

Sensitivity metric
A custom metric normalizing the average amount by which new scenarios offset the baseline. For instance, a sensitivity metric of 0.19 would be found if, for each individual year, the net-zero scenarios shifted the normal price up or down by about 20%7.

*Note: Prices per oil barrel8 are in 2022 US dollars.

  # Subset by benchmark
  split(paste(.$Variable)) %>%
  # Sub-subset by scenario
  lapply(\(x) { split(x, x$Scenario) }) %>%
  # Get prices for all years
  lapply(\(x) sapply(x, pull, "Value", "Year")) %>%
  # Compute the average differential
  reduce(`-`) %>% { -. } %>% mean()

value_box_value <- v1 %>%
  rp_format_proper() %>%

value_box_value <- Financials$Benchmark_Prices %>%
  filter(Unit == "US$/bbl") %>%
  mutate(Variable = Variable %>% fct_drop()) %>%
  # Subset by benchmark
  split(paste(.$Variable)) %>%
  # Sub-subset by scenario
  lapply(\(x) {split(x, x$Scenario)}) %>%
  # Get prices for all years
  lapply(\(x) sapply(x, pull, "Value", "Year")) %>%
  # Compute sensitivity by benchmark (average accumulated change in scale from baseline)
  sapply(\(x) {mean( sqrt( colSums((x[, -1]/x[, 1] - 1)^2) / nrow(x) ) )}) %>%

What do these represent?


Henry Hub
Henry Hub is the pricing point for natural gas futures traded on the New York Mercantile Exchange. The hub is located in Louisiana at the interconnection of numerous natural gas pipelines.
Nova Inventory Transfer (NIT)
The Nova Inventory Transfer, or NIT, is the pricing point used for natural gas sourced from the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin (WCSB). As the natural gas trading hub located in Alberta, NIT is the commercial mechanism that overlays the Nova Gas Transmission Ltd. (NGTL) pipeline network, and is connected to several export markets and storage.
Source: EF20239, with added footnotes.

Henry Hub-NIT price differential
A measure of natural gas market favorability. The interpretation for Canada is that heavy crude oil can be sold more easily if the differential is narrow10, indicating a demand strong enough to offset heavy refining costs.

Sensitivity metric
A custom metric normalizing the average amount by which new scenarios offset the baseline. For instance, a sensitivity metric of 0.27 would be found if, for each individual year, the net-zero scenarios shifted the normal price up or down by 27%11.

title: Dashboard
text:
_

Primary Demand


Demand$Primary_Demand %>%
  filter(Year > 2022) %>%
  # Omitting totals to avoid double-counting
  filter(Variable != "Total",
         Region != "Canada") %>%
  to_hierarchical_plot(orientation = "v", color_mode = "gradient",
                       title = "Generation-Side Demand (Petajoules)",
                       factor_cols = c("Variable", "Region"),
                       value_col = "Value",
                       frame_col = "Scenario")








End-Use Demand


Demand$End_Use_Demand %>%
  filter(Year > 2022) %>%
  # Omitting totals to avoid double-counting
  filter(Sector != "Total End-Use",
         Variable != "Total End-Use",
         Region != "Canada") %>%
  to_hierarchical_plot(orientation = "v", color_mode = "gradient",
                       title = "Consumption-Side Demand (Petajoules)",
                       factor_cols = c("Sector", "Variable", "Region"),
                       value_col = "Value",
                       frame_col = "Scenario")

e.g., Below 15 US$/bbl↩︎
The reference point is updated year by year. The metric is also averaged across new scenarios. Example: If one scenario halves the baseline and another keeps it unchanged, the metric is 0.25. This is because half of the baseline was subtracted by one of the two scenarios, adding 0.25 to the pooled metric.↩︎
1 bbl is ~0.159 m³↩︎

The reference point is updated year by year. The metric is also averaged across new scenarios. Example: If one scenario halves the baseline and another keeps it unchanged, the metric is 0.25. This is because half of the baseline was subtracted by one of the two scenarios, adding 0.25 to the pooled metric.↩︎
1 MMBtu is ~1.055 Gigajoules↩︎
